topics.title: Three-phase induction motor drive using stator voltage control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL408 - POWER ELECTRONICS AND DRIVES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 15
> - **Topic:** Three-phase induction motor drive using stator voltage control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Three-phase induction motor stator voltage control** is a scalar (open-loop) speed-control technique in which the **RMS value of the stator line voltage** applied to a squirrel-cage induction motor is varied by means of a **three-phase AC voltage controller** (also called a *thyristor voltage regulator* or *soft-starter circuit*), while the **supply frequency is held constant** at the grid value (typically **50 Hz** in India).

The three-phase AC-AC converter comprises three pairs of anti-parallel connected **SCRs (Silicon Controlled Rectifiers)** or three **TRIACs**, one pair per phase, connected between the three-phase AC mains and the stator terminals. By phase-angle modulating the firing angle $\alpha$ of these bidirectional semiconductor switches, the effective fundamental RMS voltage reaching the motor stator is reduced, thereby controlling the air-gap flux and electromagnetic torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEEL408 Module 15):**
> Stator voltage control is the simplest, lowest-cost method of speed control of a squirrel-cage induction motor. It is *open-loop*, *scalar*, and uses an *AC voltage controller* as the power-processing stage. The motor torque is proportional to the square of the applied stator voltage, so speed variation is achieved indirectly through torque reduction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a flat highway at a steady 60 km/h. The accelerator pedal controls the *engine torque*. Now imagine the road starts to slope upwards — the same accelerator position will not give you 60 km/h anymore; the speed drops.

In an induction motor, **frequency** is analogous to *road grade (load)* and **voltage** is analogous to the *accelerator pedal*. We keep the "road" (frequency = 50 Hz) fixed, and we squeeze the "accelerator" (reduce voltage). The motor develops less torque and settles at a lower speed. The semiconductor AC voltage controller acts exactly like a "variable dimmer switch" that chops a controllable portion of each AC half-cycle before delivering it to the motor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world example:** Domestic ceiling fans, kitchen exhaust fans, and small water-pump drives. The familiar regulator box at home for a fan is a single-phase version of this exact principle; in the lab, we extend it to all three phases.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Value / Symbol | Remarks |
|----------|------------------------|---------|
| Line frequency (India) | $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$ | Held constant in voltage control |
| Synchronous speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Depends on number of poles $P$ |
| Firing angle range | $0 \leq \alpha \leq \pi$ | Conduction window shrinks as $\alpha$ increases |
| Per-phase supply RMS | $V_s$ | Phase voltage of the 3-φ source |
| Fundamental RMS output | $V_{o1} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | Resistive load reference |
| Torque (low-slip region) | $T \propto V_s^2$ | The critical "square-law" relationship |
| Slip at max torque | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}}$ | **Independent of $V_s$** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Stator voltage is the only control variable.**
The supply frequency $f$ is locked to the grid (50 Hz). Therefore, synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ is a *fixed constant* determined solely by the motor's pole count $P$.

**Step 2 — Three-phase AC voltage controller acts as a fast electronic tap-changer.**
Six thyristors (two per phase) are anti-parallel connected. By delaying the firing instant by an angle $\alpha$ within each half-cycle, the controller *chops* the leading portion of every voltage half-wave. The RMS voltage delivered to the motor drops as $\alpha$ increases.

**Step 3 — Air-gap flux is reduced.**
Induction-motor air-gap flux $\Phi \propto V_s / f$. With $f$ constant, $\Phi \propto V_s$. Reducing $V_s$ therefore reduces the air-gap flux.

**Step 4 — Electromagnetic torque reduces as the square of voltage.**
The developed electromagnetic torque is given by

$$T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + \left(\omega_s L_{lr}\right)^2}$$

For small slips ($s \ll s_{max}$), the rotor resistance term dominates, simplifying to

$$T_e \approx \dfrac{3 s \, V_s^2}{\omega_s R_r}$$

This shows that **for a given slip $s$, torque is proportional to $V_s^2$**. Halving the voltage reduces available torque to one-quarter — this is why voltage control works only for *low-starting-torque loads* such as fans and centrifugal pumps whose load torque grows as the square of speed.

**Step 5 — Slip at maximum torque is unchanged.**
The slip at which maximum (pull-out) torque occurs is $s_{max} = R_r / X_{lr}$, which is **independent of stator voltage**. As $V_s$ is reduced, the entire torque-slip curve shifts *downward* (maximum torque drops) without any horizontal shift. This is a *signature* of voltage control.

**Step 6 — New steady-state operating point is found at the intersection of motor torque curve and load torque curve.**
Since motor torque is reduced but $s_{max}$ is unchanged, the new intersection occurs at a *higher slip* — i.e., **lower running speed**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ | Synchronous mechanical speed | rpm |
| 2 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Per-unit slip | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi f}{P/2} = \dfrac{4 \pi f}{P}$ | Synchronous electrical angular speed | rad/s |
| 4 | $T_e = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$ | Exact developed torque | N·m |
| 5 | $T_e \approx \dfrac{3 s V_s^2}{\omega_s R_r}$ | Low-slip approximation ($s \ll s_{max}$) | N·m |
| 6 | $s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}}$ | Slip at maximum torque | dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_s^2}{2 \omega_s X_{lr}}$ | Maximum (pull-out) torque | N·m |
| 8 | $V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\pi} + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | Fundamental RMS output (resistive load) | V |
| 9 | $V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi} \left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | Alternative form of the same expression | V |
| 10 | $PF \approx \dfrac{\sqrt{1 - \alpha/\pi + \sin 2\alpha/(2\pi)}}{\sqrt{2} \cos(\alpha/2)}$ | Displacement + distortion power factor | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the $V_s^2$ relation matters:** Voltage control inherently changes motor *torque capability*, not speed directly. Any load whose torque demand rises faster than the square of speed (e.g., constant-torque conveyors) will *stall* the motor at reduced voltage.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stator voltage control is the *workhorse* of low-power, low-cost industrial and domestic drives. The most prevalent applications are:

- **Ceiling fans and table fans** (the "regulator box" at home is a TRIAC-based single-phase version).
- **Exhaust fans, blowers, and centrifugal pumps** where load torque $\propto N^2$.
- **Domestic refrigerator compressors** (with soft-start and run-capacitor variants).
- **HVAC air-handling units** in large buildings.
- **Small machine-tool spindle drives** for low-torque applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not use it for cranes or elevators?** These need *constant torque* over the entire speed range, but voltage control gives $T \propto s$, so torque collapses at low speeds. **V/f control (VFD)** must be used instead.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Torque-Slip Relation (Exact Form)

**Starting point — per-phase equivalent circuit of the induction motor referred to stator:**

At slip $s$, the rotor branch has resistance $R_r/s$ (this captures the electromechanical energy conversion). The magnetising branch is approximated to be at the supply terminals (approximate equivalent circuit, valid for moderate-size motors).

Applying voltage divider on the rotor branch:

$$I_r = \dfrac{V_s}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right) + j X_{lr}}$$

Air-gap power (power transferred across the air gap, per phase) is:

$$P_{ag,\phi} = \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$$

Total air-gap power (3 phases):

$$P_{ag} = 3 \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$$

Mechanical power developed equals air-gap power minus rotor copper loss, but a more direct route is to divide by synchronous speed (in mechanical rad/s) to obtain developed torque:

$$T_e = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$$

This is the **exact developed-torque expression**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Low-Slip Approximation

For $s \ll s_{max}$, the rotor leakage reactance term $X_{lr}^2$ is much smaller than $(R_r/s)^2$, so we can drop it:

$$\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 + X_{lr}^2 \approx \left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2$$

Substituting:

$$T_e \approx \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot \dfrac{R_r}{s}}{\left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot s}{R_r}$$

Regrouping to expose the proportionality:

$$T_e \approx \left(\dfrac{3 V_s^2}{\omega_s R_r}\right) \cdot s$$

This shows that, in the operating (low-slip) region, torque varies *linearly* with slip, with a slope that is itself proportional to $V_s^2$. Doubling $V_s$ quadruples the slope.

### 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Torque and Corresponding Slip

Starting from the exact torque expression, differentiate $T_e$ with respect to $s$ and set to zero.

$$\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}}$$

Substitute $s_{max}$ back into the torque equation to obtain $T_{max}$:

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_s^2}{2 \omega_s X_{lr}}$$

**Key observation:** $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$ but $s_{max}$ does **not depend on $V_s$**. This is what makes the torque-speed curves of voltage control a *family of curves that share the same $s_{max}$*.

### 3.4 Derivation of Fundamental RMS Output Voltage of a 1-φ AC Voltage Controller (Resistive Load)

Each half-cycle of a phase-controlled waveform conducts from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi$. The RMS value over one full period is:

$$V_{or}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2 \theta \; d\theta$$

Using the identity $\sin^2 \theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$:

$$V_{or}^2 = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \left(1 - \cos 2\theta\right) d\theta$$

$$V_{or}^2 = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \theta - \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

$$V_{or}^2 = \dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

Since $V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$, we have $V_m^2 = 2 V_s^2$:

$$V_{or}^2 = \dfrac{V_s^2}{\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

$$\boxed{V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi} \left( \pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This resistive-load expression is the **textbook benchmark**. With the motor (highly inductive) load, the conduction extends beyond $\pi$ due to stored inductive energy, and the analysis requires solving transcendental equations for the extinction angle $\beta$.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 3-φ, 4-pole, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor has $R_r = 0.5\ \Omega$/phase and $X_{lr} = 2.5\ \Omega$/phase. Rated phase voltage is 230 V. Compute (a) synchronous speed, (b) slip at maximum torque, (c) maximum torque, (d) operating slip when the stator voltage is reduced to 75 % of rated and load torque is 60 % of rated maximum torque.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Synchronous speed:

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm}$$

Synchronous angular speed (electrical):

$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 50}{2} = 50 \pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(b)** Slip at maximum torque:

$$s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}} = \dfrac{0.5}{2.5} = 0.2 \;(20\%)$$

**(c)** Maximum torque at rated voltage:

$$T_{max,\text{rated}} = \dfrac{3 \times 230^2}{2 \times 157.08 \times 2.5} = \dfrac{158700}{785.4} \approx 202.06 \text{ N·m}$$

**(d)** At $V_s = 0.75 \times 230 = 172.5$ V, the new maximum torque becomes:

$$T_{max,0.75} = T_{max,\text{rated}} \times (0.75)^2 = 202.06 \times 0.5625 \approx 113.66 \text{ N·m}$$

Required load torque:

$$T_{load} = 0.6 \times 202.06 \approx 121.24 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T_{load} > T_{max,0.75}$, the motor **will not be able to drive the load at 75 % voltage** — it will stall. This illustrates the **inherent limitation of stator voltage control**: voltage cannot be reduced too far without losing the ability to drive the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Take-away for KTU viva:** Voltage control is *load-sensitive*. The same percentage voltage reduction is feasible for fan-type ($T_L \propto N^2$) loads but impossible for constant-torque loads.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the principle of stator voltage control method for speed control of a three-phase induction motor. Why is it suitable only for fan and pump type loads?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The stator voltage control method varies the RMS value of the stator supply voltage of a three-phase induction motor at **constant supply frequency**, using a phase-controlled AC voltage controller (three pairs of anti-parallel SCRs or three TRIACs). The synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ remains fixed; the air-gap flux $\Phi \propto V_s/f$ reduces with $V_s$, which in turn reduces the developed electromagnetic torque (since $T \propto V_s^2$ in the low-slip region). The new steady-state operating point is reached at a higher slip, i.e., a **lower running speed**.

It is suitable only for fan and pump loads because their load torque characteristic is $T_L \propto N^2$ (quadratic), which closely matches the available motor torque at reduced voltage. For constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists), the motor will stall when voltage is reduced, so voltage control cannot be used.

*(1 mark — principle, 1 mark — equation/proportionality, 1 mark — load-type justification.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is the effect of stator voltage control on the maximum torque and the slip at maximum torque of an induction motor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The maximum (pull-out) torque of a three-phase induction motor is given by

$$T_{max} = \dfrac{3 V_s^2}{2 \omega_s X_{lr}}$$

Since $T_{max} \propto V_s^2$, a reduction in stator voltage **reduces the maximum torque in proportion to the square of the voltage**.

The slip at which maximum torque occurs is

$$s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}}$$

This expression contains **no $V_s$ term**, so $s_{max}$ is **independent of the stator voltage** and remains unchanged under voltage control.

Consequently, the family of torque-slip curves at different voltages all pass through the *same slip value* $s_{max}$ but at *different peak torque values* — a *vertically compressed* family of curves.

*(1 mark — $T_{max}$ expression, 1 mark — $V_s^2$ proportionality, 1 mark — $s_{max}$ independent of $V_s$.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** With the help of a neat power circuit diagram, explain the operation of a three-phase AC voltage controller used for stator voltage control of a three-phase squirrel-cage induction motor. Discuss the role of firing angle $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the RMS value of the output voltage of a single-phase AC voltage controller with resistive load, and plot the variation of output RMS voltage with firing angle $\alpha$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

A three-phase AC voltage controller consists of **three pairs of anti-parallel connected SCRs** (or three TRIACs), one pair in each of the three supply lines, connected between the three-phase 50 Hz AC mains and the stator terminals of the squirrel-cage induction motor.

*Power Circuit Description:*

- Phase A: SCR $T_{A1}$ conducts positive half-cycles; SCR $T_{A2}$ (anti-parallel) conducts negative half-cycles.
- Phase B and Phase C: analogous pairs.
- The cathodes/grounds of all three phases meet at the motor stator terminals.
- A common firing circuit generates six gate pulses per cycle, with firing angle $\alpha$ referenced to the natural commutation point of each phase voltage.

*Role of Firing Angle $\alpha$:*

- Each SCR is fired at an instant delayed by $\alpha$ from the zero-crossing of its phase voltage.
- For $0 \le \alpha \le \pi/2$, part of the half-cycle is chopped; the RMS voltage delivered to the motor is reduced.
- For $\alpha > \pi/2$, only a small pulse of each half-cycle is delivered, and discontinuous conduction occurs.
- The **greater the $\alpha$, the lower the RMS voltage**, and the lower the developed torque and the steady-state speed.

*Mark Split:* Circuit description — 3 marks; firing-angle role — 2 marks; speed/torque linkage — 2 marks.

**(b) — 7 marks**

The derivation is shown in Section 3.4 of these notes. Re-summarized for the answer script:

$$V_{or}^2 = \dfrac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2 \theta \, d\theta$$

$$= \dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} (1 - \cos 2\theta) \, d\theta$$

$$= \dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \theta - \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2} \right]_{\alpha}^{\pi}$$

$$= \dfrac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

With $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_s$:

$$\boxed{V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi} \left( \pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right)}}$$

*Plot:* The output RMS voltage $V_{or}$ is a monotonically decreasing function of $\alpha$:

- At $\alpha = 0$: $V_{or} = V_s$ (full conduction, full voltage).
- At $\alpha = \pi/2$: $V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{1/2 + 1/\pi} \approx 0.84 V_s$.
- At $\alpha = \pi$: $V_{or} = 0$ (no conduction, no voltage).

*Mark Split:* Setting up the integral — 2 marks; simplification — 3 marks; final boxed result + qualitative plot description — 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the developed torque of a three-phase induction motor as a function of slip and stator voltage. Show that the slip at maximum torque is independent of stator voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz squirrel-cage induction motor has $R_r = 0.4\ \Omega$ and $X_{lr} = 1.6\ \Omega$ per phase. The rated phase voltage is 220 V. Compute (i) synchronous speed, (ii) slip at maximum torque, (iii) maximum torque, and (iv) the operating slip at $V_s = 0.6 V_{rated}$ when the load torque is 80 % of rated maximum torque. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

Use the approximate per-phase equivalent circuit (magnetising branch shifted to the source). At slip $s$, the rotor branch presents impedance $(R_r/s) + jX_{lr}$.

Rotor current per phase:

$$I_r = \dfrac{V_s}{(R_r/s) + jX_{lr}}$$

Magnitude-squared:

$$\vert I_r \vert^2 = \dfrac{V_s^2}{(R_r/s)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$$

Air-gap power per phase:

$$P_{ag,\phi} = \vert I_r \vert^2 \cdot (R_r/s) = \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r/s)}{(R_r/s)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$$

Total 3-phase air-gap power and developed torque:

$$T_e = \dfrac{P_{ag}}{\omega_s} = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2 \cdot (R_r/s)}{(R_r/s)^2 + X_{lr}^2}$$

*Slip at Maximum Torque:*

Differentiate $T_e$ w.r.t. $s$ and set to zero. The optimum condition is:

$$\dfrac{dT_e}{ds} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \left(\dfrac{R_r}{s}\right)^2 = X_{lr}^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}}$$

The expression $s_{max} = R_r / X_{lr}$ contains no $V_s$, confirming that **the slip at maximum torque is independent of stator voltage**. *(7 marks — full distribution.)*

**(b) — 7 marks**

*Given:* $P = 6$, $f = 50$ Hz, $R_r = 0.4\ \Omega$, $X_{lr} = 1.6\ \Omega$, $V_{rated} = 220$ V (phase).

**(i) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\omega_s = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 50}{3} \approx 104.72 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(ii) Slip at maximum torque:**

$$s_{max} = \dfrac{R_r}{X_{lr}} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.6} = 0.25 \;(25\%)$$

**(iii) Maximum torque at rated voltage:**

$$T_{max,\text{rated}} = \dfrac{3 \times 220^2}{2 \times 104.72 \times 1.6} = \dfrac{145200}{335.10} \approx 433.30 \text{ N·m}$$

**(iv) At $V_s = 0.6 \times 220 = 132$ V:**

New maximum torque:

$$T_{max,0.6} = 433.30 \times (0.6)^2 = 433.30 \times 0.36 \approx 155.99 \text{ N·m}$$

Required load torque:

$$T_{load} = 0.8 \times 433.30 \approx 346.64 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T_{load} = 346.64$ N·m $\gg T_{max,0.6} = 155.99$ N·m, **the motor cannot drive the load at 60 % voltage and will stall**.

*Comment:* This confirms the major limitation of stator voltage control — it is unsuitable for constant-torque loads because reducing voltage quadratically reduces the available torque.

*Mark Split:* (i) 1.5 marks; (ii) 1 mark; (iii) 1.5 marks; (iv) 2 marks; comment 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\omega_s$ in the denominator of the torque expression** — examiners award partial credit only if the slip-vs-torque proportionality is correctly framed.
> 2. **Stating "$T \propto V$"** instead of "$T \propto V^2$" in the low-slip region. The squaring is *the* single most important fact in this experiment.
> 3. **Ignoring the harmonic content** of the AC voltage controller output. The motor receives a non-sinusoidal voltage; this causes additional rotor copper losses, torque pulsations, and acoustic noise. Mention it for full credit.
> 4. **Conflating stator voltage control with V/f control.** They are *different* methods. Voltage control fixes $f$; V/f control maintains *constant V/f*. Many students mix these up in viva.
> 5. **Plotting torque-speed curves** without explicitly noting that all curves share the same $s_{max}$ — a key identifying feature of voltage control.
> 6. **Not writing units** for $T_{max}$, $N_s$, etc. Examiners explicitly deduct for missing SI units.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Method:** Vary stator RMS voltage at *constant supply frequency* using a 3-phase AC voltage controller (six anti-parallel SCRs or three TRIACs).
- **Torque proportionality:** $T_e \propto V_s^2$ in the low-slip (operating) region.
- **Slip at max torque:** $s_{max} = R_r / X_{lr}$ — **independent of $V_s$**.
- **Maximum torque:** $T_{max} = 3 V_s^2 / (2 \omega_s X_{lr})$ — varies as $V_s^2$.
- **Synchronous speed:** $N_s = 120 f / P$ — fixed by grid frequency and pole count.
- **Steady-state speed reduction:** Achieved *indirectly*, by lowering torque and letting the slip grow until motor torque matches load torque.
- **Suitable loads:** Quadratic-torque loads — fans, blowers, centrifugal pumps.
- **Unsuitable loads:** Constant-torque loads (conveyors, hoists, compressors) — motor stalls.
- **Power circuit:** 6 thyristors (3 pairs of anti-parallel) plus 6-pulse firing circuit.
- **Output:** Non-sinusoidal, rich in odd harmonics of order $6k \pm 1$ for balanced 3-phase operation.
- **Advantages:** Simple, low-cost, no feedback required, works with any standard squirrel-cage motor.
- **Disadvantages:** Limited speed range (typically 70–100 % of rated speed for fan loads), poor power factor at low speed, harmonic heating, no constant-torque capability.
- **Higher alternative:** **V/f (VFD) control** — maintains $\Phi$ constant by raising $V_s$ in proportion to $f$, giving constant torque over the full speed range.
- **Key formula for AC controller output (resistive load):**

$$V_{or} = V_s \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$$

- **Lab measurement practice (PCEEL408 Exp 15):** Vary the firing angle $\alpha$ in steps (e.g., $0°, 30°, 60°, 90°, 120°$), record motor no-load and loaded speed, line current, line voltage, and power; plot speed vs $\alpha$, current vs $\alpha$, and torque vs speed for each $\alpha$ setting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single phase unidirectional/bidirectional boost PWM rectifier with near unity power factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEEL408 - POWER ELECTRONICS AND DRIVES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 15 > - **Topic:** Single phase unidirectional/bidirectional boost PWM rectifier with near unity power factor # MODULE EXPERIMENT 15: SINGLE PHASE BOOST PWM RECTIFIER ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)** > A **Single-Phase Boost PWM Rectifier** is a forced-commutated power electronic converter that uses active semiconductor switches (IGBTs/MOSFETs) in conjunction with a boost inductor, diode bridge (or bidirectional switch leg), and DC-link capacitor to convert single-phase AC supply into a regulated, higher DC output voltage. By employing **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** at high switching frequency with sinusoidal reference current tracking, the input current is forced to be sinusoidal and in-phase with the supply voltage, achieving a **near unity power factor (≈ 0.99)** and **low Total Harmonic Distortion (THD < 5%)**, in compliance with IEEE 519 and IEC 61000-3-2 standards. ### 1.2 Classification of the Rectifier | Type | Power Flow | Active Switches | Typical Use | |------|------------|-----------------|-------------| | **Unidirectional Boost PWM Rectifier** | AC → DC only | One IGBT + 4 diodes (semi-controlled) | SMPS, PFC front-end, telecom rectifiers | | **Bidirectional Boost PWM Rectifier** | AC ↔ DC (dual) | 4 IGBTs (full H-bridge) | Regenerative drives, EV chargers, UPS | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Intelligent Water Tap" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Analogy: The Smart Tap and Sponge** > Imagine an old **leaky tap** (conventional diode bridge) — water (current) gushes out only in pulses, splashing everywhere (high THD, low PF ≈ 0.6–0.7), and you cannot control the flow direction. > Now replace it with a **smart electronic tap** (PWM-controlled IGBT) that: > - Senses the exact water pressure (input voltage waveform), > - Adjusts the opening **hundreds of times per second** (high-frequency PWM), > - Pushes water smoothly in a **sinusoidal stream in-phase with the pressure** (unity PF), > - And can also **reverse flow** when needed (bidirectional). > > The **boost inductor (L)** acts like a **smoothing sponge** that absorbs the pulsed current and releases it as a clean sine wave. The **DC-link capacitor (C)** acts like an **overhead water tank** that maintains constant output pressure (regulated DC voltage). ### 1.4 Why PWM Rectifier? Limitations of Conventional Diode Rectifier > [!WARNING] > **Conventional Diode Bridge Problems (Why We Need PWM Rectifier):** > 1. **Poor Power Factor (0.6 – 0.7)** — current is highly peaky, lagging. > 2. **High THD (60% – 80%)** — rich in 3rd, 5th, 7th harmonics. > 3. **No Output Voltage Regulation** — V_dc depends on load and supply. > 4. **Unidirectional Only** — cannot feed regenerative energy back. > 5. **Large Filter Size** — needs bulky LC filter to smooth current. ### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standards > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Metrics for this Experiment:** > - **Input Voltage (V_s,rms):** 110 V / 230 V (single-phase, 50 Hz) > - **Output DC Voltage (V_dc):** 380 V – 400 V (typical boost ratio) > - **Switching Frequency (f_sw):** **10 kHz – 20 kHz** (above audible range) > - **Target Power Factor:** **≥ 0.99** (near unity) > - **Target THD (i):** **< 5%** > - **Standard Compliance:** **IEEE 519**, **IEC 61000-3-2** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of Boost PWM Rectifier The boost PWM rectifier operates on the principle of **indirect current control** using a boost inductor as the energy storage element. ### 2.1.1 Working Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **AC Supply Sensing:** The line voltage v_s(t) = V_m sin(ωt) is sensed using a voltage transducer. 2. **Reference Current Generation:** A sinusoidal reference current i*_s(t) = I*_m sin(ωt) is generated, in-phase with v_s(t). This ensures unity power factor. 3. **Current Error Computation:** The error ε(t) = i*_s(t) − i_s(t) is fed to a current controller (hysteresis / PI / PR). 4. **PWM Generation:** The controller produces gating pulses for the IGBT such that the actual current tracks the reference. 5. **Boost Action:** When switch is ON, inductor stores energy (L di/dt = v_s); when OFF, inductor + source charge the DC-link capacitor (L di/dt = v_s − V_dc). 6. **Steady State:** V_dc > V_peak (boost operation), output is regulated DC. ### 2.2 Circuit Topologies #### Topology A: Unidirectional Boost PWM Rectifier (Current Fed) **Components:** Diode Bridge (D1–D4) + Boost Inductor (L) + IGBT (S) + Freewheel Diode (D) + DC Capacitor (C) + Load (R). #### Topology B: Bidirectional Boost PWM Rectifier (Voltage Fed Full Bridge) **Components:** 4 IGBTs (S1–S4) in H-bridge + Boost Inductor (L) + DC Capacitor (C) + Load (R). Energy can flow either AC→DC (rectifier) or DC→AC (inverter). ### 2.3 PWM Control Strategies | Strategy | Principle | Advantages | Disadvantages | |----------|-----------|------------|---------------| | **Hysteresis Current Control (HCC)** | Compares i_s with a hysteresis band ±h | Simple, fast response, no carrier needed | Variable switching frequency | | **SPWM (Sinusoidal PWM)** | Compares sine reference with high-freq triangular carrier | Fixed f_sw, predictable spectrum | Slower dynamic response | | **SVPWM (Space Vector PWM)** | Uses space vector modulation (1-φ equivalent: DPWM) | Better DC utilization, lower switching loss | Complex implementation | | **Predictive / Model-Based Control** | Uses system model to predict next state | Optimal switching, low THD | High computational load | ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **CHEAT SHEET — All Key Equations (KEEP THIS HANDY FOR EXAM)** | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | |---|----------|---------|-------------| | 1 | Source Voltage | $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ | Single-phase sinusoidal supply | | 2 | Reference Current (UPF) | $i^{*}_s(t) = I^{*}_m \sin(\omega t)$ | In-phase with v_s for unity PF | | 3 | Boost Output Voltage | $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_m}{1 - D}$ | D = duty ratio of IGBT | | 4 | Boost Inductor Value | $L = \dfrac{V_m \cdot D}{\Delta i_{L} \cdot f_{sw}}$ | Sized for current ripple Δi_L | | 5 | DC-Link Capacitor | $C = \dfrac{I_{dc}}{2 \omega \cdot \Delta V_{dc}}$ | Sized for voltage ripple ΔV_dc | | 6 | Input Power (1-φ) | $P_{in} = V_s I_s \cos\phi$ | With UPF, cosφ = 1 | | 7 | Output Power | $P_{out} = V_{dc} I_{dc} = \dfrac{V^{2}_{dc}}{R}$ | DC load power | | 8 | Power Factor (Total) | $PF = \cos\phi_1 \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^{2}_i}}$ | With current distortion | | 9 | THD of Current | $THD_i = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{\infty} I^{2}_h}}{I_1} \times 100\%$ | Harmonic distortion | | 10 | Displacement Factor | $DF = \cos\phi_1$ | Phase angle between V_1 and I_1 | | 11 | Distortion Factor | $d = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^{2}}}$ | Due to harmonics only | | 12 | Modulation Index | $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{control}}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$ | SPWM control parameter | | 13 | Hysteresis Band | $h = \dfrac{V_{dc} - V_m}{2 L f_{sw}} \cdot T$ | Half-width of current band | | 14 | Crest Factor | $CF = \dfrac{I_{peak}}{I_{rms}}$ | For sine: 1.414 | | 15 | RMS Current | $I_{s,rms} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_s \cdot PF}$ | Used for design | | 16 | Inductor Energy | $E_L = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}_{L,peak}$ | Stored in boost inductor | | 17 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}}$ | Practical: 92% – 97% | | 18 | Power Balance (lossless) | $V_s I_s = V_{dc} I_{dc}$ | Conservation of energy | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL DESIGN RULE:** For **Boost** operation, the condition $V_{dc} > V_m$ **MUST** hold. Hence, $D = 1 - \dfrac{V_m}{V_{dc}} < 1$. If $V_{dc} \leq V_m$, the converter fails to boost and PF collapses. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where Boost PWM Rectifiers Are Used:** > 1. **Electric Vehicle (EV) On-board Chargers** — bidirectional, regenerates braking energy. > 2. **Telecom SMPS / Server Power Supplies** — 48 V DC bus from 230 V AC with PFC. > 3. **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** — Active Front End (AFE) for regenerative braking. > 4. **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — bidirectional AC-DC link. > 5. **Renewable Energy Grid Interface** — solar inverter front stage. > 6. **Traction Locomotive Drives** — regenerates to overhead line. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Boost Output Voltage vs Duty Cycle **Starting Point:** Apply volt-second balance on the boost inductor over one switching period $T_s$. **Step 1 — During switch ON (duration DT_s):** The switch S is closed. Diode D is reverse-biased. The full source voltage appears across L. $$ v_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L} $$ Integrating over the ON-time: $$ \Delta I_{L,on} = \frac{V_s \cdot D T_s}{L} $$ **Step 2 — During switch OFF (duration (1−D)T_s):** Switch S opens. The inductor current flows through diode D into the DC-link. The voltage across L is: $$ v_L = L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_s - V_{dc} $$ Integrating over the OFF-time: $$ \Delta I_{L,off} = \frac{(V_s - V_{dc})(1-D) T_s}{L} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Volt-Second Balance (Steady State):** In steady state, net change in inductor current over one period = 0. $$ \Delta I_{L,on} + \Delta I_{L,off} = 0 $$ $$ \frac{V_s \cdot D T_s}{L} + \frac{(V_s - V_{dc})(1-D) T_s}{L} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification:** $$ V_s \cdot D + (V_s - V_{dc})(1-D) = 0 $$ $$ V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_{dc} + V_{dc} D = 0 $$ $$ V_s - V_{dc}(1 - D) = 0 $$ $$ \boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** As D → 1, V_dc → ∞ (theoretically). Practical boost ratio limited by component ratings. For UPF operation in AC, we use **average V_s** ≈ 0.9·V_s,rms. ## 3.2 Derivation: Power Factor with Harmonic Distortion **Step 1 — Define Displacement Power Factor:** The phase angle φ_1 between fundamental voltage V_1 and fundamental current I_1: $$ DPF = \cos\phi_1 $$ **Step 2 — Define Distortion Factor:** Ratio of fundamental RMS to total RMS: $$ d = \frac{I_1}{\sqrt{I^{2}_1 + I^{2}_2 + I^{2}_3 + \cdots}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^{2}_i}} $$ **Step 3 — Total Power Factor:** $$ PF = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{V \cdot I_1 \cos\phi_1}{V \cdot I_{rms}} = \cos\phi_1 \cdot \frac{I_1}{I_{rms}} $$ Substituting: $$ \boxed{PF = \frac{\cos\phi_1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^{2}_i}}} $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Example:** Suppose φ_1 = 0° (in-phase) and THD = 5% = 0.05: $$ PF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 0.05^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.0025}} = \frac{1}{1.00125} \approx 0.9988 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Even with 5% THD, the PF remains near unity (0.9988). This is why high-quality PWM rectifiers achieve PF > 0.99 easily. ## 3.3 Derivation: Boost Inductor Design **Step 1 — Allowed Current Ripple:** Design for a peak-to-peak ripple of, say, 20% of peak current: $$ \Delta I_L = 0.2 \cdot I_{L,peak} $$ **Step 2 — Inductor Voltage Equations:** $$ L \frac{di_L}{dt} = V_s \quad \text{(during ON, time } DT_s\text{)} $$ Therefore: $$ L = \frac{V_s \cdot D \cdot T_s}{\Delta I_L} = \frac{V_s \cdot D}{\Delta I_L \cdot f_{sw}} $$ **Step 3 — Numerical Computation:** Given: V_s = 230 V (rms), V_m = 325 V, V_dc = 400 V, f_sw = 20 kHz, ΔI_L = 2 A. Compute duty ratio: $$ D = 1 - \frac{V_s}{V_{dc}} = 1 - \frac{325}{400} = 1 - 0.8125 = 0.1875 $$ Compute inductance: $$ L = \frac{325 \times 0.1875}{2 \times 20000} = \frac{60.94}{40000} = 1.523 \text{ mH} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** A **1.5 mH** inductor is sufficient for the given design. Round up to standard value: **1.5 mH or 2 mH**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Rectifier Types | Parameter | Diode Rectifier | Thyristor (SCR) Rectifier | **Boost PWM Rectifier** | |-----------|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------------| | Power Factor | 0.6 – 0.7 lagging | 0.3 – 0.7 (firing angle dependent) | **0.98 – 0.99 (near unity)** | | THD (i) | 60% – 80% | 30% – 50% | **< 5%** | | Output Voltage Regulation | No (≈ 1.35 V_s) | Yes (firing angle) | **Yes (closed loop)** | | Bidirectional Power Flow | No | No (semi-bridge possible) | **Yes (full-bridge)** | | Switching Frequency | Line freq (50 Hz) | Line freq (50 Hz) | **10–20 kHz** | | Inductor Size | Large (line freq) | Large (line freq) | **Small (high f_sw)** | | Efficiency | 90% – 95% | 92% – 96% | **93% – 97%** | | Complexity | Very Low | Low | **High (DSP/Microcontroller)** | | Cost | Low | Low-Medium | **High** | ## 3.5 Hysteresis Current Control — Working Math **Step 1 — Hysteresis Band Definition:** Upper limit: $i^{*}_{s,upper} = I^{*}_m \sin(\omega t) + h$ Lower limit: $i^{*}_{s,lower} = I^{*}_m \sin(\omega t) - h$ **Step 2 — Switching Logic:** $$ \text{If } i_s < i^{*}_{lower} \Rightarrow S = ON \text{ (current rises)} $$ $$ \text{If } i_s > i^{*}_{upper} \Rightarrow S = OFF \text{ (current falls)} $$ **Step 3 — Switching Frequency:** $$ f_{sw} = \frac{V_{dc}}{4 h L} \left[1 - \left(\frac{V_m \sin\omega t}{V_{dc}}\right)^2 \right] $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** f_sw varies with time (max at ωt = 0 and π, min at ωt = π/2). Average f_sw is designed ≈ 20 kHz for 2% ripple. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB ACS[AC Supply 230V 50Hz] --> VS[Voltage Sensor] ACS --> IL[Boost Inductor L] IL --> HBR[H-Bridge 4x IGBT] HBR --> DCC[DC Link Capacitor C] DCC --> LD[DC Load R] VS --> REF[Reference Current Generator i*sin wt] CT[Current Sensor CT] --> COMP[Comparator + Hysteresis Band] REF --> COMP COMP --> GD[Gating Driver Circuit] GD --> HBR DCC --> VFB[Voltage Feedback Vdc] VFB --> PI[PI Controller] PI --> REF ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Unidirectional Boost PWM Rectifier ```mermaid flowchart LR AC[AC Source vs] -->|terminal| L1[Inductor L] L1 -->|node A| DB[Diode Bridge D1-D4] DB -->|node B| SW[IGBT Switch S] SW -->|node C| N[Neutral / DC-] DB -->|node D| DFW[Freewheel Diode D] DFW -->|node E| CDC[DC Capacitor C] CDC -->|positive rail| VDC[+ Vdc] CDC -->|negative rail| N VDC --> RL[Load R] RL --> N ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Hysteresis Current Control ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> SwitchON SwitchON: Switch ON
Current Rising
di/dt positive SwitchON --> SwitchOFF: is exceeds upper band SwitchOFF: Switch OFF
Current Falling
di/dt negative SwitchOFF --> SwitchON: is falls below lower band SwitchON --> [*] SwitchOFF --> [*] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Bidirectional PWM Rectifier Power Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Mode1[Rectifier Mode AC to DC] A1[AC Source] -->|forward| L1[Inductor L] L1 -->|PWM charging| CB[DC Link Capacitor] CB -->|supplies| R1[DC Load] end subgraph Mode2[Inverter Mode DC to AC] A2[DC Source / Battery] -->|discharges| CB2[DC Link] CB2 -->|PWM inversion| L2[Inductor L] L2 -->|sine current| A1b[AC Grid] end ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Flowchart: Closed-Loop Control Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TB START([Start]) --> M1[Measure vs, is, Vdc] M1 --> M2[Compute error ev = Vdc_ref - Vdc] M2 --> M3[PI Controller ev to Im] M3 --> M4[Generate reference is_ref = Im sin wt] M4 --> M5[Measure actual is] M5 --> M6{Is less than is_ref + h?} M6 -->|Yes| M7[Turn ON IGBT] M6 -->|No| M8[Turn OFF IGBT] M7 --> M9[Update Switching] M8 --> M9 M9 --> M1 ``` ## 4.6 Pinout & Hardware Connection Table | Pin No. | Signal | Source → Destination | Function | |---------|--------|----------------------|----------| | 1 | V_s (PT secondary) | PT → Controller ADC | Voltage sensing | | 2 | I_s (CT secondary) | CT → Controller ADC | Current sensing | | 3 | V_dc (Divider) | Voltage Divider → ADC | DC link feedback | | 4 | Gate Pulse PWM | DSP → Driver → IGBT Gate | Switching control | | 5 | Enable | DSP → Driver EN | Safety interlock | | 6 | Fault | Driver → DSP | Overcurrent/OV protection | | 7 | +15V | SMPS → Driver IC | Driver power | | 8 | GND | Common | Reference ground | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define "near unity power factor" in the context of a boost PWM rectifier. What is the typical numerical value targeted?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Near unity power factor means the input current drawn by the rectifier is almost perfectly in-phase with the supply voltage, and is nearly sinusoidal. This is achieved by forcing the input current to track a sinusoidal reference in-phase with v_s(t) using PWM. The typical targeted value is **PF ≥ 0.99** (with THD < 5% and displacement angle φ_1 ≈ 0°). This complies with IEEE 519 standards and minimizes reactive power demand from the grid, reducing distribution losses. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State any three advantages of a PWM boost rectifier over a conventional diode bridge rectifier.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Near Unity Power Factor (0.99):** PWM rectifier forces sinusoidal in-phase current, eliminating reactive power. 2. **Low Input Current THD (< 5%):** Unlike diode bridge (THD 60–80%), the high-frequency PWM shoves harmonics to very high frequencies, easy to filter. 3. **Regulated DC Output Voltage:** Closed-loop control maintains V_dc constant irrespective of load and supply variations. 4. *(Bonus — optional 4th)* Bidirectional power flow possible with full-bridge topology. --- ## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Choice-Based) ### **Question A: 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Explain the working of a single-phase unidirectional boost PWM rectifier with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the DC output voltage in terms of duty ratio.** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Working Principle & Circuit** **Model Solution:** The single-phase unidirectional boost PWM rectifier consists of a **diode bridge rectifier (D1–D4)**, a **boost inductor (L)** at the AC side, an **active IGBT switch (S)** with anti-parallel or series freewheel diode, and a **DC-link capacitor (C)** feeding a resistive load (R). **Mode 1 — Switch ON (duration DT_s):** The IGBT S is turned ON. The diode bridge output is shorted through S. The AC source drives current through L, storing energy. The DC capacitor C supplies the load in isolation. The inductor current rises linearly: $$ v_s = L \frac{di_L}{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{v_s}{L} > 0 $$ **Mode 2 — Switch OFF (duration (1-D)T_s):** The IGBT S is OFF. The inductor, which opposes sudden change in current, drives current through the freewheel diode D into the DC-link capacitor. Now the source plus inductor both charge the capacitor: $$ v_s - V_{dc} = L \frac{di_L}{dt} \Rightarrow \frac{di_L}{dt} = \frac{v_s - V_{dc}}{L} $$ Since V_dc > v_s, di/dt < 0, current falls. **Steady State:** Energy stored in L in mode 1 = Energy released in mode 2. Capacitor maintains constant V_dc. **PWM Control:** The switching duty D is modulated sinusoidally such that the input current i_s(t) becomes sinusoidal and in-phase with v_s(t), achieving unity power factor. (3 marks for circuit diagram, 2 marks for modes, 2 marks for PWM concept.) #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of V_dc** **Model Solution (Step-by-Step):** Applying **volt-second balance** on L over one switching period T_s = 1/f_sw: $$ \int_0^{T_s} v_L(t) \, dt = 0 $$ Switch ON for DT_s, Switch OFF for (1-D)T_s: $$ V_s \cdot D T_s + (V_s - V_{dc})(1 - D) T_s = 0 $$ Dividing by T_s: $$ V_s D + (V_s - V_{dc})(1 - D) = 0 $$ Expanding: $$ V_s D + V_s - V_s D - V_{dc} + V_{dc} D = 0 $$ Cancelling V_s D terms: $$ V_s - V_{dc}(1 - D) = 0 $$ Solving for V_dc: $$ \boxed{V_{dc} = \frac{V_s}{1 - D}} $$ For single-phase AC, V_s is replaced by the peak value V_m (or averaged V_s over half-cycle ≈ 0.9 V_s,rms) to give the DC output: $$ V_{dc} = \frac{V_m}{1 - D_{avg}} $$ This proves the **boost** characteristic: V_dc > V_s. (2 marks for volt-second balance, 3 marks for algebraic expansion, 2 marks for final boxed result and interpretation.) --- ### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Describe the operation of a single-phase bidirectional boost PWM rectifier using a full H-bridge. Explain how near unity power factor is achieved using current control. List four advantages of this topology.** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Full H-Bridge Bidirectional Operation** **Model Solution:** A **bidirectional boost PWM rectifier** replaces the diode bridge with **four IGBT switches (S1, S2, S3, S4)** in an H-bridge configuration, with the boost inductor L at the AC side and DC-link capacitor C at the DC side. **Mode 1 — Rectifier Operation (AC → DC):** - During positive half-cycle: S1, S4 ON; S2, S3 OFF. Current flows from source → L → S1 → C (charging) → load → S4 → source. - During negative half-cycle: S2, S3 ON; S1, S4 OFF. Current flows reverse. - DC bus voltage V_dc > V_m, current is in-phase with v_s. **Mode 2 — Inverter Operation (DC → AC):** - For regenerative braking (e.g., in EV): DC source (battery/super-cap) feeds back to grid. - Gating is reversed such that the converter operates as a voltage-source inverter (VSI) with L as filter. - Current is 180° out-of-phase with v_s (negative power flow). **PWM Modulation:** Bipolar or unipolar SPWM is applied with reference current i*_s(t) = I*_m sin(ωt + φ). In rectifier mode φ = 0° (UPF). In inverter mode φ = 180°. (2 marks circuit, 3 marks mode explanation, 2 marks for UPF logic.) #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: UPF Achievement & Advantages** **Model Solution:** **Achieving Near Unity Power Factor:** The input current is forced to be sinusoidal and in-phase with the input voltage through a **closed-loop current control** scheme: 1. **Reference Generation:** A PLL (Phase-Locked Loop) locks to v_s(t). A reference i*_s(t) = I*_m sin(ωt) is generated with zero phase shift. 2. **Current Sensing:** Hall-effect CT senses i_s(t) in real-time. 3. **Error Computation:** ε(t) = i*_s(t) − i_s(t). 4. **Hysteresis Comparator:** If ε > +h → S OFF; if ε < −h → S ON. This forces i_s to track i*_s within band ±h. 5. **Result:** i_s is sinusoidal, in-phase with v_s → PF ≈ 1.0, THD < 5%. **Four Advantages:** 1. **Bidirectional power flow** — enables regenerative braking. 2. **Near unity PF (0.99)** — minimal reactive power draw. 3. **Low THD (< 5%)** — meets IEEE 519 without bulky filters. 4. **Regulated DC bus** — constant V_dc despite load/supply variation. 5. *(Optional 5th)* Small filter size due to high f_sw (10–20 kHz). (3 marks UPF explanation with block diagram, 2 marks advantages enumeration, 2 marks for waveform/THD comparison.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing unidirectional with bidirectional topology** — unidirectional uses 1 IGBT + diode bridge; bidirectional uses 4 IGBTs. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to state V_dc > V_s condition** — Boost fails otherwise. Examiners specifically look for this assertion. (−1 mark) > 3. **Skipping the volt-second balance step in derivation** — directly writing V_dc = V_s/(1−D) without derivation loses 3–4 marks. Always show the integral equation. > 4. **Not mentioning closed-loop control for UPF** — merely stating "PF is unity" without describing current tracking/hysteresis/PWM is incomplete. (−2 marks) > 5. **Forgetting to label axes in waveforms** — if you draw v_s, i_s, V_dc, gate pulses, label time on x-axis and volts/amps on y-axis. Unlabelled waveforms = 0 marks in KTU. > 6. **Using wrong modulation index formula** — m_a = V_control / V_tri (peak ratio), not RMS. (−1 mark) > 7. **Ignoring IEEE 519 standard mention** — bonus mark available if you cite compliance. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Module Experiment 15** - ⚡ **Boost PWM Rectifier** uses high-frequency PWM to force sinusoidal input current. - ⚡ **Unity PF** = input current in-phase (φ = 0°) and sinusoidal with v_s. - ⚡ **Boost Equation:** $V_{dc} = \dfrac{V_s}{1 - D}$ (always V_dc > V_s). - ⚡ **Unidirectional** = 1 IGBT + 4 diodes + L + C (one-way AC→DC). - ⚡ **Bidirectional** = 4 IGBTs H-bridge + L + C (two-way AC↔DC). - ⚡ **Hysteresis Control** = variable f_sw, simplest implementation. - ⚡ **SPWM** = fixed f_sw, sinusoidal reference vs triangular carrier. - ⚡ **Switching frequency range:** **10 kHz – 20 kHz** (above audible). - ⚡ **Targeted PF:** ≥ **0.99** | **Targeted THD:** < **5%**. - ⚡ **Standards:** **IEEE 519**, **IEC 61000-3-2**. - ⚡ **Inductor sizing:** $L = \dfrac{V_s \cdot D}{\Delta i_L \cdot f_{sw}}$. - ⚡ **Capacitor sizing:** $C = \dfrac{I_{dc}}{2 \omega \cdot \Delta V_{dc}}$. - ⚡ **Power factor with harmonics:** $PF = \dfrac{\cos\phi_1}{\sqrt{1 + THD^{2}_i}}$. - ⚡ **Modulation index:** $m_a = \dfrac{\hat{V}_{control}}{\hat{V}_{tri}}$. - ⚡ **Bidirectional enables regenerative braking** in EV and VFDs. - ⚡ **Efficiency practical range:** **93% – 97%**. - ⚡ **Crest factor for ideal sine input = 1.414** — verifies current quality. - ⚡ **Duty ratio for boost** = $D = 1 - \dfrac{V_s}{V_{dc}}$ (always < 1). - ⚡ **Applications:** EV chargers, telecom SMPS, AFE drives, UPS, solar inverters. - ⚡ **Disadvantages:** Complex control, needs DSP/Microcontroller, EMI from high f_sw.

topics.title: V/F control of three-phase induction motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEEL408 - POWER ELECTRONICS AND DRIVES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 15
> - **Topic:** V/F control of three-phase induction motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**V/F Control (also called Volts-per-Hertz Control)** of a three-phase induction motor is a **scalar (magnitude-only) speed control strategy** in which the stator voltage $V$ and supply frequency $f$ are varied simultaneously such that their **ratio $V/f$ is held approximately constant** over the operating range. This keeps the air-gap flux $\phi$ in the motor approximately constant, thereby preserving the torque-producing capability of the machine.

Mathematically, the governing principle is:

$$\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \phi \approx \text{constant}$$

This is the **most widely used open-loop speed control technique** for industrial AC drives, implemented using a **PWM Voltage Source Inverter (VSI)** in the vast majority of commercial Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The V/F experiment is a *core lab experiment* in the PED Lab. Students are expected to (a) wire the inverter–motor setup, (b) vary frequency from 5 Hz to 50 Hz, (c) record terminal voltage at each frequency, (d) plot the V/f curve, and (e) measure no-load/loaded speed and verify $N_s = 120 f / P$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cruise-Control Car on a Flyover

Imagine driving a car on a flyover. The accelerator controls the engine's **strength** (voltage) and the gearbox determines how **fast the engine rotates** (frequency). If you change the gear (frequency) without touching the accelerator, the car's pulling force (torque) drops — there isn't enough "push" to move it. But if you press the accelerator (raise voltage) **in proportion** every time you upshift, the pulling force stays the same and the car smoothly speeds up.

The induction motor behaves the same way:
- **Frequency** → controls **synchronous speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$.
- **Voltage** → controls **air-gap flux** (and therefore torque).
- The smart drive always raises voltage **in step** with frequency → V/f = constant → smooth speed control without losing torque.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight (Why V/f Constant?):** The per-phase induced EMF in the stator is $E \approx 4.44 \, f \, N \, \phi \, K_w$. If $f$ changes, $\phi$ would change inversely. Since torque $T \propto \phi \, I_r$, keeping $\phi$ constant is essential to keep torque capability constant. Therefore, $V \approx E \propto f \cdot \phi$, and to keep $\phi$ fixed, $V$ must rise in proportion to $f$.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value (3-$\phi$ IM) | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Base frequency (India) | **50 Hz** | $f_{base}$ |
| Base voltage (line) | **415 V** | $V_{LL,base}$ |
| Synchronous speed at 50 Hz, 4-pole | **1500 rpm** | $N_{s,base}$ |
| Synchronous speed at 50 Hz, 2-pole | **3000 rpm** | $N_{s,base}$ |
| Synchronous speed formula | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | rpm |
| Electrical angular frequency | $\omega_s = 2 \pi f$ | rad/s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Induction Motor Speed Needs Variable Frequency

The speed of an induction motor is governed by the synchronous speed of the rotating magnetic field, which is set by the supply frequency $f$ and the number of poles $P$:

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \quad [\text{rpm}]$$

The rotor runs slightly below $N_s$ with a slip $s$:

$$s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}, \quad N_r = N_s (1 - s)$$

To control $N_r$ smoothly from near zero up to several times base speed, we **must vary $f$**, because $P$ is a fixed machine construction parameter.

## 2.2 The Air-Gap Flux Constraint

From Faraday's law applied to the stator winding:

$$E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w \, \phi$$

Neglecting stator impedance drop, the applied terminal voltage $V_1 \approx E_1$, so:

$$\phi = \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w}$$

For $\phi$ to stay constant, the ratio $V_1 / f$ must stay constant. This is the **single most important relationship** in V/F control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Operating Regions of the Drive:**
> 1. **Region 1 (Below base frequency, 0 to $f_{base}$):** Constant $V/f$ → Constant flux → **Constant torque region**.
> 2. **Region 2 (Above base frequency, $f > f_{base}$):** Voltage clamped at $V_{rated}$ (insulation limit) → Flux falls as $1/f$ → **Constant power (field-weakening) region**.
> 3. **Region 3 (Very high frequency):** Both constant power and constant current limits are exceeded → **Series (natural) region**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Exam & Lab Viva**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Region of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = 120 f / P$ | rpm | All |
| 2 | Rotor (actual) speed | $N_r = N_s(1 - s)$ | rpm | All |
| 3 | Slip | $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$ | per-unit (0 to 1) | All |
| 4 | Air-gap flux | $\phi = V / (4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} K_w)$ | Wb | All |
| 5 | Electromagnetic torque | $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2_{th}}{(R_{th} + R'_r/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X'_r)^2} \cdot \dfrac{R'_r}{s}$ | N·m | All |
| 6 | Mechanical power developed | $P_m = T \, \omega_r = \dfrac{2 \pi N_r T}{60}$ | W | All |
| 7 | V/f ratio (rated) | $(V/f)_{rated} = V_{rated} / f_{rated}$ | V/Hz | Region 1 |
| 8 | Per-phase voltage (line-to-line to phase) | $V_{ph} = V_{LL} / \sqrt{3}$ | V | All |
| 9 | Electrical angular frequency | $\omega_s = 2 \pi f$ | rad/s | All |
| 10 | Slip speed | $N_{slip} = N_s - N_r$ | rpm | All |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Rule Applied:** Vertical bars inside table cells are rendered using $\vert$ or $\mid$ to avoid breaking the table syntax (e.g., absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$, not $\vert x \vert$ with raw pipes).

## 2.4 The Equivalent Circuit Behind V/F Control

A simplified per-phase Thevenin equivalent as seen by the rotor:

- Stator: $R_1$, $X_1$ (stator resistance & leakage reactance)
- Magnetizing branch: $X_m$ (magnetizing reactance) — this is where the **flux is established**
- Rotor branch: $R'_r / s$ (slip-dependent effective resistance), $X'_r$ (rotor leakage reactance)
- Thevenin voltage: $V_{th} = V_1 \cdot \dfrac{j X_m}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$
- Thevenin impedance: $Z_{th} = \dfrac{j X_m (R_1 + j X_1)}{R_1 + j(X_1 + X_m)}$

**How V/F Control Acts on This Circuit:** By raising $V_1$ in lockstep with $f$, the induced flux $\phi$ (and therefore the magnetizing current through $X_m$) stays at the rated value. This keeps the rotor current and torque production healthy across the entire speed range.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial pumps, fans, blowers, compressors** — energy savings of 20–50% over dampers.
- **Conveyor belts, mixers, extruders** — soft start, soft stop, controlled acceleration.
- **HVAC systems** — V/F drives are now mandatory in large buildings (BEE Star rating in India).
- **Electric vehicles (low-cost variants)** — V/F control of the auxiliary motor.
- **Textile, paper, sugar mills** — speed-synchronized process lines.
- **Servo-grade V/F + slip regulation** is the workhorse of the global AC drives market, competing with vector and DTC drives in cost-sensitive applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for Synchronous Speed

The rotating magnetic field completes one mechanical revolution in $P/2$ electrical cycles, and there are $f$ electrical cycles per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of electrical cycles per second} &= f \\
\text{Number of mechanical revolutions per second} &= \frac{f}{P/2} = \frac{2f}{P} \\
\text{Number of mechanical revolutions per minute} &= \frac{2f}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 f}{P}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} \;\; \text{rpm}}$$

This is the foundation of any frequency-controlled drive. Doubling the frequency doubles the synchronous speed.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Air-Gap Flux from the EMF Equation

For a stator phase winding with $N_{ph}$ turns, winding factor $K_w$, and flux $\phi$ per pole:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w \, \phi \\
\phi &= \frac{E_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w} \quad \text{(Step 1: rearrange for } \phi \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming $V_1 \approx E_1$ (neglecting the small stator impedance drop at moderate loads):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi &\approx \frac{V_1}{4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} \, K_w} \\
\text{If } \frac{V_1}{f} &= \text{constant} = k, \quad \text{then} \quad \phi = \frac{k}{4.44 \, N_{ph} \, K_w} = \text{constant} \\
\therefore \quad \boxed{\frac{V}{f} = \text{constant} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \phi = \text{constant}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Torque Equation in the V/F Mode

The electromagnetic torque developed by an induction motor, derived from the Thevenin equivalent, is:

$$T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2_{th} \cdot (R'_r / s)}{(R_{th} + R'_r / s)^2 + (X_{th} + X'_r)^2}$$

To find the **maximum (pull-out) torque**, differentiate $T$ with respect to slip $s$ and set $dT/ds = 0$. The result is the well-known expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{max} &= \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2_{th}}{R_{th} + \sqrt{R^2_{th} + (X_{th} + X'_r)^2}} \\
s_{max} &= \frac{R'_r}{\sqrt{R^2_{th} + (X_{th} + X'_r)^2}} \\
\text{Since } \omega_s &= 2 \pi f \quad \text{and} \quad V_{th} \propto f \quad \text{(in the constant V/f region),} \\
V^2_{th} / \omega_s &= V^2_{th} / (2 \pi f) = \frac{(k f)^2}{2 \pi f} = \frac{k^2 f}{2 \pi} \\
\therefore \quad T_{max} &\propto f \quad \text{(at low frequencies, the stator drop becomes non-negligible, so we add } V_{boost} \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical Fix — Voltage Boost (Low-Frequency Compensation):**

At very low frequencies, the stator $I_1 R_1$ drop is comparable to $V_1$, so the actual flux dips and torque capability falls. Drives compensate by adding a small **voltage boost** $\Delta V$ at low frequencies:

$$V_{ref}(f) = \underbrace{V_{boost}}_{\text{constant offset}} + k \cdot f$$

A typical boost profile is shown in SECTION 4.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Flow and Slip Power Recovery (Conceptual)

The air-gap power $P_g$ splits into mechanical power $P_m$ and rotor copper loss $P_{r,cu}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_g &= 3 \cdot I^2_r \cdot \frac{R'_r}{s} \\
P_m &= (1 - s) P_g \\
P_{r,cu} &= s \cdot P_g \\
P_{in} &= P_g + P_{s,cu} + P_{core} \\
P_{out} &= P_m - P_{F\&W} - P_{stray}
\end{aligned}
$$

V/F control does not change this fundamental power-flow structure, but it does allow $s$ to settle at a *desired* value through the constant $V/f$ law, which is what gives the motor its rated torque.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — V/F Behaviour Across the Operating Envelope

| Speed Range | Frequency | Voltage | V/f | Flux $\phi$ | Torque Capability | Power | Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 – $f_{base}$ | Increases linearly | Increases linearly (with $V_{boost}$) | **Constant** | Constant | **Constant** | Increases linearly | Region 1 (CT) |
| $f_{base}$ – $2 f_{base}$ | Increases | Clamped at $V_{rated}$ | Decreases $\propto 1/f$ | Decreases $\propto 1/f$ | Decreases $\propto 1/f$ | **Constant** | Region 2 (CP) |
| $> 2 f_{base}$ | Limited by bearings & inverter | Clamped at $V_{rated}$ | Decreases | Decreases sharply | Decreases sharply $\propto 1/f^2$ | Decreases | Region 3 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of Open-Loop V/F Control

```mermaid
    B --> C1[Voltage Magnitude Command V ref]
```

## 4.3 V/f Voltage Profile with Low-Frequency Boost

```mermaid
graph LR
```

**Interpretation of the Profile:**
- **A → B (0–3 Hz):** A fixed $V_{boost} \approx 5\text{–}30$ V is applied so that the motor develops rated torque even at standstill/creep.
- **B → D (3–50 Hz):** Linear region, $V = k f$, where $k = 415/50 = 8.3$ V/Hz.
- **D → E (50–100 Hz):** Voltage clamped at 415 V (insulation & IGBT ceiling); $V/f$ falls, flux weakens, motor enters constant-power region.
- **E → F (above 100 Hz):** Series region — both power and torque decay.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PCEEL408, 2024 Scheme]`**

### Q1. Define V/F control of a three-phase induction motor. Why is the ratio maintained constant?
**Model Answer:**
V/F control is a scalar speed-control technique in which the stator voltage $V$ is varied in proportion to the supply frequency $f$, keeping the ratio $V/f$ constant. This is necessary because the air-gap flux $\phi \propto V/f$, and torque is proportional to flux. By keeping $\phi$ constant, the motor's torque-producing capability is preserved across the entire speed range, enabling smooth speed control from near-zero to base speed. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. What is voltage boost in a V/F drive? When is it applied?
**Model Answer:**
Voltage boost is a small additional DC offset (typically 5–30 V on a 415 V drive) added to the V/F reference at low frequencies (below ~3–5 Hz) to compensate for the stator $I_1 R_1$ voltage drop, which becomes significant at low $f$. Without boost, the flux and hence the torque would fall at low speeds, making the motor unable to start or drive low-speed loads. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each)

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Jun 2024, PCEEL408 — Module Experiment 15, 2024 Scheme]`
> *(Internal choice: answer either Question A or Question B)*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the open-loop V/F control scheme of a three-phase induction motor using a PWM voltage source inverter. Show the V/f profile with and without voltage boost. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz, 415 V induction motor is controlled by a V/F drive. Calculate (i) the synchronous speed, (ii) the rotor speed at 4% slip, (iii) the new V/f ratio if the drive is set to 25 Hz, and (iv) the voltage that must be applied at 25 Hz. The motor runs at 2% slip at this frequency. Find the rotor speed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **Block Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4, Figure 4.2. The three building blocks are: *Frequency reference → V/f function generator → Sine PWM modulator → Gate driver → Two-level VSI → Induction motor → Load*.
2. **V/F Profile:** Plot a straight line $V = k f$ from origin, with a small low-frequency boost offset (typically 3–30 V at 0–3 Hz), and a horizontal clamp above $f_{base}$ (50 Hz).
3. **Operation:** The frequency reference $f_{ref}$ is converted by the V/f function generator into a proportional voltage reference $V_{ref}$ and a phase-sequenced sinusoidal reference. A high-frequency triangular carrier (typically 2–15 kHz) compares with the sinusoidal reference to generate PWM pulses that drive the IGBT gates of the VSI.
4. **Why V/F Constant:** Because $\phi \propto V/f$, holding $V/f$ constant keeps $\phi$ constant, which keeps torque capability constant — the defining feature of the constant-torque region.
5. **Low-frequency boost:** Necessary because $I_1 R_1$ drop is comparable to $V_1$ at low $f$, so without boost, flux (and torque) would collapse.
6. **Region above base frequency:** $V$ clamped at 415 V, $V/f$ falls, flux falls → field-weakening / constant-power region.
7. **Conclusion:** Open-loop V/F control provides simple, low-cost speed control suitable for pumps, fans, and conveyors. **(7 marks distributed as: block diagram – 2, V/f profile – 2, explanation – 2, regions & boost – 1)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

Given: $P = 4$ poles, $f_1 = 50$ Hz, $V_{LL} = 415$ V.

**(i) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \, f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \; \text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Rotor speed at 4 % slip:**

$$N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \; \text{rpm}$$

**(iii) V/f ratio:**

$$\left(\frac{V}{f}\right)_{rated} = \frac{415}{50} = 8.3 \; \text{V/Hz}$$

At $f_2 = 25$ Hz, the new V/f ratio (in the linear region, ignoring boost) remains:

$$\left(\frac{V}{f}\right)_{new} = 8.3 \; \text{V/Hz}$$

**(iv) Voltage at 25 Hz and rotor speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LL,new} &= 8.3 \times 25 = 207.5 \; \text{V} \\
N_{s,new} &= \frac{120 \times 25}{4} = 750 \; \text{rpm} \\
N_{r,new} &= N_{s,new} (1 - s) = 750 \times (1 - 0.02) = 750 \times 0.98 = 735 \; \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:** (i) 1500 rpm, (ii) 1440 rpm, (iii) 8.3 V/Hz, (iv) $V_{LL} = 207.5$ V, $N_r = 735$ rpm. **(7 marks — 1 each for steps (i)–(iv) and 3 for the final computed values)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** Draw and explain the torque–slip characteristic of a three-phase induction motor at three different frequencies using a V/F drive. Show that the maximum torque is independent of frequency in the constant V/f region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-phase, 6-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 3%. Calculate (i) the synchronous speed, (ii) the full-load rotor speed, (iii) the rotor frequency at full load, and (iv) the speed of the motor when the drive frequency is reduced to 30 Hz with the same slip. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

1. **Torque Equation Recap:** $T = \dfrac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \dfrac{V^2_{th} \cdot (R'_r / s)}{(R_{th} + R'_r/s)^2 + (X_{th} + X'_r)^2}$
2. **Maximum Torque:** Setting $dT/ds = 0$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{3}{2 \omega_s} \cdot \frac{V^2_{th}}{R_{th} + \sqrt{R^2_{th} + (X_{th} + X'_r)^2}}$$

3. **Substitute $V_{th} \propto f$ and $\omega_s = 2 \pi f$ in the constant V/f region:**

$$\frac{V^2_{th}}{\omega_s} = \frac{(k f)^2}{2 \pi f} = \frac{k^2 f}{2 \pi}$$

If the stator impedance drops are neglected (or compensated by voltage boost), then $R_{th}$ and $X_{th}$ are approximately independent of $f$. The reactance $X_{th} + X'_r = 2 \pi f (L_{th} + L'_r)$ does scale with $f$ — but in the simplified analysis (or in the high-frequency limit), $T_{max}$ becomes **approximately constant** in the constant V/f region.

4. **Graph Sketch:** Three T–s curves at $f_1 < f_2 < f_3$ all share the **same $T_{max}$** and have peaks shifted to higher slips. As $f$ increases, the stable operating point moves to higher $N_r$ while maintaining the same pull-out torque. **(7 marks — 2 for equation, 2 for derivation, 2 for graph, 1 for conclusion)**

#### Model Solution to (b)

Given: $P = 6$ poles, $f_1 = 50$ Hz, $s_{FL} = 0.03$.

**(i) Synchronous speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{6} = 1000 \; \text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Full-load rotor speed:**

$$N_r = 1000 (1 - 0.03) = 970 \; \text{rpm}$$

**(iii) Rotor (slip) frequency:**

$$f_r = s \cdot f = 0.03 \times 50 = 1.5 \; \text{Hz}$$

**(iv) Speed at 30 Hz with same slip:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{s,new} &= \frac{120 \times 30}{6} = 600 \; \text{rpm} \\
N_{r,new} &= 600 (1 - 0.03) = 600 \times 0.97 = 582 \; \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:** (i) 1000 rpm, (ii) 970 rpm, (iii) 1.5 Hz, (iv) 582 rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\sqrt{3}$:** When converting line-to-line voltage to per-phase voltage, students often write $V_{ph} = V_{LL}$ and lose 1 mark. Correct: $V_{ph} = V_{LL}/\sqrt{3}$.
> 2. **Ignoring voltage boost:** When asked for the V/f profile, students draw a straight line through the origin. The correct profile has a **small offset (boost) at 0–3 Hz** to compensate for stator drop.
> 3. **Confusing rotor frequency with supply frequency:** Rotor frequency is $f_r = s \cdot f$, not $f$ itself. At full-load slip, $f_r \approx 1$–$3$ Hz only.
> 4. **Stating $T_{max}$ is exactly constant:** It is **approximately constant** in the V/f region; the exact expression contains $X_{th} + X'_r$ which scales with $f$, so the simplification is valid only when boost compensates for the $I_1 R_1$ drop and the leakage reactance effect is small.
> 5. **Missing the field-weakening region:** When $f > f_{base}$, voltage is clamped at the rated value — students often wrongly extend the linear V/f line.
> 6. **In the lab record:** A common deduction is for **not plotting the V/f curve** or for not tabulating $V$, $f$, and $N_r$ in a single observation table.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam & Lab Viva**

- **Core formula:** $N_s = 120 f / P$; $N_r = N_s (1 - s)$.
- **V/f law:** $V/f = \text{constant} \Leftrightarrow \phi = \text{constant} \Leftrightarrow$ constant torque.
- **Three regions:** Constant-Torque (0 to $f_{base}$), Constant-Power ($f_{base}$ to $2 f_{base}$), Series (above $2 f_{base}$).
- **Voltage boost:** Small fixed offset at low frequency to overcome $I_1 R_1$ drop.
- **EMF equation:** $E_1 = 4.44 \, f \, N_{ph} K_w \phi$ — memorize the constant **4.44**.
- **Phase voltage:** $V_{ph} = V_{LL} / \sqrt{3}$ — for a 415 V (line) Indian supply, $V_{ph} = 240$ V.
- **Lab setup:** 3-$\phi$ variac → MCB → VFD (rectifier + DC link + VSI) → 3-$\phi$ induction motor → loading arrangement → tachometer.
- **Observations to record:** $f$ (Hz), $V_{LL}$ (V), $N_r$ (rpm) — and compute $N_s$, slip $s$, V/f ratio for each row.
- **Graphs to plot:** (a) $V$ vs $f$ (should be a straight line above 5 Hz with a small offset below), (b) $N_r$ vs $f$ (should be linear), (c) $T$–$N$ characteristic.
- **Typical V/f ratio for Indian drives:** $415/50 = 8.3$ V/Hz (line-to-line, per Hz).
- **PWM strategy used:** Sine-triangle PWM (SPWM) at 2–15 kHz carrier; Space Vector PWM (SVPWM) is the modern upgrade that gives 15% higher DC-bus utilization.
- **Slip frequency:** $f_r = s \cdot f$ — small at rated load (1–3 Hz), important for slip-regulation / closed-loop V/F.
- **Equipment safety:** Never run the motor below 5 Hz for long; ensure forced air cooling; DC-link discharge time is ~5 minutes after power-off.
- **Viva favourites:** Why V/f constant? → To keep flux constant. Why boost? → To overcome stator drop at low frequency. Why voltage clamp above 50 Hz? → Insulation & IGBT ceiling. What is the synchronous speed of a 4-pole, 50 Hz motor? → 1500 rpm.
